summary: Tractate Yoma daf 78b covers: Cork shoe on Yom Kippur: is it a real shoe? Permitted or forbidden?; Young children permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kippur; A king wears shoes even on Yom Kippur for honor; dispute about kings; Eating a date-bulk on YK is prohibited; one who eats this amount is liable.

display.whats: Cork shoe on Yom Kippur: is it a real shoe? Permitted or forbidden?
display.hint: The Gemara asks whether a cork sandal counts as a 'shoe' for the YK prohibition. Since cork provides only minimal protection, the question is whether it falls under the prohibition or is permitted.
display.title: The Cork Sandal: Real Shoe or Not?

learning.ahaMoment: Cork may be permitted on YK: it is too minimal to be the 'shoe' that signals dignity, and the prohibition targets the dignity marker.
learning.memoryAnchor: Cork sandal - too minimal for the prohibition: the shoe YK targets is a dignity marker, not any foot covering.
learning.learnerQuestion: Does a sandal made of cork qualify as the 'shoe' prohibited on Yom Kippur?
learning.coreTension: Between the functional definition of footwear (cork protects the foot minimally) and the dignity-based definition the YK prohibition envisions (a shoe as a status marker).
learning.coreMove: The Gemara raises the cork sandal question to probe the definition of 'shoe' in the prohibition. Since cork provides only minimal protection and minimal dignity, it may not qualify as the dignified footwear the Torah targets. The prohibition reveals it is about status, not foot coverage.

takeaway.text: The YK shoe prohibition targets dignified footwear as a social status marker; minimal foot coverings that provide little protection and no social dignity may not meet the definition of 'shoe' the prohibition envisions.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume any foot covering is prohibited; the Gemara's analysis of the cork sandal reveals the criterion is dignity, not material.

argumentFlow.label: Cork and reed sandals are permitted on Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yitzchak bar Nachmani testified that Rabbi Yehoshua ben Levi went out in cork sandals on Yom Kippur and ruled them no different from a public fast day, where they are also permitted. Other Sages confirm the practice: Rav Yehuda, Abaye, Rava, and Rabba bar Rav Huna each went out in non-leather footwear or wrapped their feet, none counting as a forbidden shoe.

argumentFlow.label: Abaye: the wooden prosthesis shows comfort is forbidden
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rami bar Chama objects from the wooden prosthetic leg, which Rabbi Meir permits on Shabbat but which all agree is forbidden on Yom Kippur. Abaye explains the Yom Kippur ban is due to the comforting rags inside the prosthesis, suggesting that any comfort, not only a shoe, is forbidden.

argumentFlow.label: Rava refutes Abaye: only shoes, not comfort generally
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rava rejects Abaye: rags do not turn a non-shoe into a shoe, and comforts other than wearing shoes are not forbidden on Yom Kippur. Rabba bar Rav Huna's wrapped feet prove rags are permitted, and the baraita's later clause about a receptacle for rags shows the first clause did not involve rags at all.

argumentFlow.label: Rava's own account of the prosthesis dispute
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rava concludes that all agree a prosthetic leg is a shoe; the Shabbat dispute is only whether to decree against wearing it lest it slip off and be carried four cubits. Rabbi Yosei decrees such a prohibition, Rabbi Meir does not, but this has nothing to do with comfort on Yom Kippur.

theological: The shoe prohibition targets protection and comfort - a cork sandal's minimal pr
narrative: Cork shoe on Yom Kippur: is it a real shoe? Permitted or for

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of cork sandal context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners conclude from the cork and reed sandals that the Yom Kippur prohibition bans only leather shoes.
misconceptions.correction: Rava frames the rule as targeting whatever functions as a shoe, while permitting other comforts like wrapped feet. Cork and reed sandals are permitted not because they are non-leather but because they do not provide the comfort of a real shoe.

visualizableElements.name: Cork shoe on Yom Kippur: is it a real shoe? Permit

quizSeeds.question: What is Abaye's argument that the Yom Kippur ban on a wooden prosthesis is about comfort, and how does Rava refute it?
quizSeeds.answer: Abaye says the prosthesis is forbidden because of the comforting rags inside it, implying comfort itself is banned. Rava refutes this: rags do not make a non-shoe into a shoe, only shoes are forbidden, and Rabba bar Rav Huna's permitted wrapped feet plus the baraita's structure show the first clause had no rags at all.

quizSeeds.question: According to Rava, what is the actual subject of the Shabbat dispute over the prosthetic leg?
quizSeeds.answer: All agree the prosthesis counts as a shoe. The dispute is only whether to decree against wearing it on Shabbat lest it slip off and one carry it four cubits in public; Rabbi Yosei makes the decree, Rabbi Meir does not.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Gemara asks whether a cork sandal counts as a 'shoe' for the YK prohibition. Since cork provides only minimal protection, the question is whether

display.whats: Young children permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kippur
display.hint: Young children are permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kippur since the affliction obligations do not apply to them.
display.title: Children and the Shoe Prohibition

learning.ahaMoment: The afflictions are the form the YK mitzvah takes - without the mitzvah, there is no prohibition. Children are exempt from the mitzvah, so they are exempt from the affliction.
learning.memoryAnchor: Children wear shoes on YK: no mitzvah obligation, no affliction prohibition.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why are young children permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kippur?
learning.coreTension: Between the communal observance of YK (creating an expectation that everyone participates) and the halakhic reality that affliction obligations apply only to those obligated in the commandments.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara reasons that the affliction obligations are the form the YK mitzvot take; without the underlying mitzvah obligation, there is no prohibition. Young children are exempt from the mitzvot of the day and therefore from the afflictions that express them.

takeaway.text: YK affliction obligations apply only to those who bear full commandment obligations; minors who are exempt from the mitzvot of the day are also exempt from the afflictions that are their expression.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think children should afflict themselves for educational reasons; the Gemara's ruling is about legal obligation, not chinuch.

argumentFlow.label: Children may do everything except wear shoes
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: A baraita permits young children to do all the otherwise prohibited Yom Kippur acts except wearing shoes. The Gemara asks why shoes differ: observers seeing a child in shoes will assume an adult put them on for him, which would imply adult transgression, whereas with bathing or oil one could assume it was done the day before.

argumentFlow.label: Why shoes cannot be attributed to the day before
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara asks why shoes too could not be assumed to have been put on yesterday, and answers that a child would not have slept in shoes overnight, as Shmuel taught that sleeping in shoes invites a taste of death, so shoes on a child must have been put on that day.

argumentFlow.label: Acts needed for growth versus those that are not
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: Since the mishna permits children's shoes ab initio, the Gemara reinterprets: the Sages decreed only against acts not needed for the child's growth, while permitting those that are. Abaye's mother taught that a child's growth requires warm water, oil, then egg with kutacha, and later vessels to break for enjoyment, as Rabba provided cracked pots.

theological: Children's exemption from YK afflictions is not just leniency - it reflects that
narrative: Young children permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kippur

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners think children are simply exempt from all Yom Kippur restrictions.
misconceptions.correction: The exemptions are calibrated to the child's needs and to how onlookers will read the act. Shoes are treated differently because they signal same-day adult involvement, and the final rule turns on whether an act is necessary for the child's growth.

visualizableElements.name: Young children permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kipp

quizSeeds.question: Why is wearing shoes singled out as forbidden for children when other Yom Kippur acts are permitted to them?
quizSeeds.answer: An onlooker seeing a child in shoes assumes an adult put them on that day, implying adult wrongdoing. With bathing or oil one could assume it was done the previous day, but a child would not have slept in shoes overnight, so shoes must have been put on during Yom Kippur itself.

quizSeeds.question: How does the Gemara reconcile permitting children's shoes ab initio with the concern about onlookers?
quizSeeds.answer: It reinterprets the rule: the Sages decreed only against acts not needed for a child's growth, while permitting those that are. Footwear and care that support the child's development are allowed, so the onlooker concern does not bar what the child genuinely needs.

sugyot.finalRuling: Young children are permitted to wear shoes on Yom Kippur since the affliction obligations do not apply to them.

display.whats: A king wears shoes even on Yom Kippur for honor; dispute about kings
display.hint: A king is permitted to wear shoes even on Yom Kippur because of the dignity of royalty; the Gemara debates whether this applies to all kings or only some.
display.title: The King's Shoes on Yom Kippur

learning.ahaMoment: The shoe prohibition is not only self-directed: if it were purely personal self-humbling, the king's public dignity would be irrelevant. The dispute itself reveals that public appearance is part of what YK regulates.
learning.memoryAnchor: King's shoes on YK: does royal dignity compete with public humility before God? The dispute is preserved.
learning.learnerQuestion: Can a king wear shoes on Yom Kippur because of royal dignity?
learning.coreTension: Between the universal application of the shoe prohibition (everyone goes barefoot on YK) and the special legal status of a king whose public dignity is a matter of law.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara debates whether kavod hamelech (royal dignity) overrides personal affliction obligations. The dispute reveals whether the shoe prohibition is purely a personal act of self-humbling or also carries a public dimension of appearing before God in humility.

takeaway.text: The question of whether royal dignity overrides the YK shoe prohibition turns on whether the prohibition is purely self-directed or also a public statement; if it has a public dimension, the king's public dignity is a genuine competing principle.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume religious prohibitions always override dignity considerations; the Gemara treats kavod hamelech as a genuine competing principle that requires resolution.

argumentFlow.label: Who permits the king and bride to wash their faces?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The mishna's view that a king and bride may wash their faces is identified with Rabbi Chananya ben Teradyon in the name of Rabbi Eliezer, against the Rabbis who forbid it. The same dispute extends to whether a new mother may wear shoes on Yom Kippur.

argumentFlow.label: Why a king and a bride are exceptions
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Eliezer's reasons: a king washes his face because 'your eyes shall see the king in his beauty,' he must look regal; a bride washes hers so she not appear repulsive to her new husband. Rav learns from a baraita that a woman is considered a bride for the first thirty days, when her appearance matters most.

argumentFlow.label: A new mother may wear shoes against the cold
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A new mother may wear shoes on Yom Kippur because the cold could make her ill in her weakened state after childbirth, so her health overrides the prohibition on wearing shoes.

argumentFlow.label: Shoes permitted where scorpions pose a danger
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Shmuel rules that one who fears scorpions while barefoot on Yom Kippur may wear shoes, since one need not place himself in danger in order to keep the affliction of going without shoes.

theological: Royal dignity is itself a religious value - even on YK, a king's honor has its o
narrative: A king wears shoes even on Yom Kippur for honor; dispute abo

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume the bare-feet and face-washing afflictions admit no exceptions.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara carves out exceptions grounded in dignity (king, bride) and in danger to health (new mother, scorpions), showing that the afflictions yield where appearance is essential to a role or where observance would threaten wellbeing.

visualizableElements.name: A king wears shoes even on Yom Kippur for honor; d

quizSeeds.question: What are the distinct reasons a king and a bride are permitted to wash their faces on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: A king must appear regal to his nation, as 'your eyes shall see the king in his beauty.' A bride washes so she will not appear repulsive to her husband at the start of their marriage, when her appearance is most critical, which the Gemara fixes at the first thirty days.

quizSeeds.question: On what principle may a new mother or someone fearing scorpions wear shoes on Yom Kippur?
quizSeeds.answer: That one need not endanger himself to observe the affliction. A new mother is weak and could fall ill from the cold, and a barefoot person facing scorpions is at risk, so in both cases health and safety override the prohibition on wearing shoes.

sugyot.finalRuling: A king is permitted to wear shoes even on Yom Kippur because of the dignity of royalty; the Gemara debates whether this applies to all kings or only s

display.whats: Eating a date-bulk on YK is prohibited; one who eats this amount is liable
display.hint: The Mishna establishes that eating a date-bulk (kotevet) on Yom Kippur is the minimum measure that creates liability; eating less than this does not incur a sin offering.
display.title: The Date-Bulk Measure for Yom Kippur Eating

learning.ahaMoment: The measure reveals the logic: YK's prohibition is about affliction-relief, so the operative threshold is the amount that actually relieves affliction.
learning.memoryAnchor: Date-bulk not olive-bulk: YK targets satisfaction, so the measure matches satisfaction.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why does Yom Kippur use a date-bulk (kotevet) as its eating measure rather than the standard olive-bulk?
learning.coreTension: Between the standard olive-bulk (k'zayit) used as the minimum eating measure in most halakhic contexts and the larger kotevet used specifically for YK eating liability.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara establishes that YK uses the larger kotevet because the prohibition targets the relief of affliction, not the mere act of consumption. Eating less than a kotevet does not provide enough satisfaction to genuinely relieve the fasting state, so it does not trigger liability.

takeaway.text: The YK eating measure is calibrated to satisfaction rather than consumption: the measure is larger than usual because the prohibition targets the relief of affliction, and only a quantity sufficient to actually relieve affliction triggers liability.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume all eating prohibitions share the same minimum measure; understanding that different prohibitions have different operative criteria explains the variation.

argumentFlow.label: The measure of eating that incurs liability
argumentFlow.speaker: Mishnah
argumentFlow.text: The mishna fixes liability for eating on Yom Kippur at the volume of a large date. Rav Pappa opens a question about this measure, which the Gemara takes up in full on the following page.

theological: The measure of liability (shiur) on YK is a date-bulk, not an egg-bulk - Yom Kip
narrative: Eating a date-bulk on YK is prohibited; one who eats this am

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Learners assume 'the volume of a large date' is a self-evident measure needing no analysis.
misconceptions.correction: Rav Pappa's question shows the measure is genuinely ambiguous, raising whether it includes the pit, which is why the Gemara devotes extended discussion to pinning it down.

visualizableElements.name: Eating a date-bulk on YK is prohibited; one who ea

quizSeeds.question: What measure of food makes one liable for eating on Yom Kippur, and what does Rav Pappa probe about it?
quizSeeds.answer: The mishna sets liability at the volume of a large date. Rav Pappa opens the question of how exactly to measure it, for instance whether the date is counted with or without its pit, which the Gemara pursues on the next page.

sugyot.finalRuling: The Mishna establishes that eating a date-bulk (kotevet) on Yom Kippur is the minimum measure that creates liability; eating less than this does not i

rashiTranslations.he: שעם. מין גומא שקורין פוי"י: בתענית צבור. הנגזר על הגשמים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: cork sandal on Yom Kippur - minimal protection raises the definitional question.

rashiTranslations.he: דתנן במסכת תענית (דף יב:) אסור בנעילת הסנדל: בדהיטני.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the definition of a sandal for purposes of the YK prohibition.

rashiTranslations.he: מין שעם: בדהוצי. סנדל של כפות תמרים: בדיבלי. של עשבים:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: children are exempt from the five afflictions on Yom Kippur.

rashiTranslations.he: יוצא בקב שלו. בשבת קב אשקצ"א: ורבי יוסי אוסר.  קסבר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: children may wear shoes since the affliction rule does not apply to them.

rashiTranslations.he: לאו מנעל הוא הואיל ואינו שוה בכל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: a king may wear shoes on Yom Kippur for the dignity of royalty.

rashiTranslations.he: ואפילו הכי שוין שאסור לצאת בו ביום
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the principle of kevod hamelech - royal dignity as a halachic category.

rashiTranslations.he: הכפורים אלמא אף על גב דלרבי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the date-bulk measure for Yom Kippur eating liability.

rashiTranslations.he: יוסי לאו מנעל הוא אפי' הכי אסיר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: the kotevet (large date) is the specific measure for YK.

rashiTranslations.he: והוא הדין לשל שעם: כתיתין.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78b.

rashiTranslations.he: חתיכות בגדים קטנות שהם נוחות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78b.

rashiTranslations.he: לו לסמוך שוקו עליהם: טמא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 78b.

rashiTranslations.he: מקבל טומאה דלאו פשוטי כלי עץ
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: הוא: מנעל הוא. הלכך ביוה"כ
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אסור אבל דהיטני ודהוצא לאו מנעל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: נינהו: מותרין בכולן. בכל הני
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דמתניתין אכילה ושתיה ורחיצה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: וסיכה: אינשי עבדו ליה. גדולים
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: עשו לו והתורה הזהירה גדולים על
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: הקטנים שלא יעשו להם בידים דבר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: האסור כדאמרינן במסכת יבמות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: (דף קיד.) לא תאכלום לא תאכילום
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: להזהיר גדולים על הקטנים: לא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אפשר מאתמול. ויהא ישן כל הלילה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: והם ברגליו: הא מותרין לכתחלה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: קתני. שאם בא לימלך אומרים לו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: האכילהו ורחוץ אותו והאי שפיר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ידע דהיום עשאו לו: מיא חמימי.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: לרחיצה: ומשחא. לסוך: ביעתא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בכותחא. ביצים בכותח: תבורי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: מאני. להפקיר לו כלים לשבר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ולמלאות תאותו: מאני גזיזי דפחרא.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: כלים סדוקים של חרם בדמים קלים:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אין מונעין תכשיטין וכו'. ברייתא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: היא ומייתינן לה בריש בתולה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: this is the baraita we bring at the start of 'A virgin is married' in Ketubot:

rashiTranslations.he: נישאת בכתובות הרי שהיה טבחו
rashiTranslations.en: 'If one's animal was slaughtered

rashiTranslations.he: טבוח ויינו מזוג ומת אביו של חתן או
rashiTranslations.en: and his wine mixed and ready, and the father of the groom or

rashiTranslations.he: אמה של כלה מכניסין את המת לחדר
rashiTranslations.en: the mother of the bride died, they bring the corpse into a chamber

rashiTranslations.he: ואת החתן ואת הכלה לחופה ובועל
rashiTranslations.en: and the groom and bride to the chuppah, and he performs

rashiTranslations.he: בעילת מצוה ופורש ואחר כך קוברין
rashiTranslations.en: the marital act, withdraws, and only afterward they bury

rashiTranslations.he: את המת ואין מונעין תכשיטין מן
rashiTranslations.en: the corpse; and they do not withhold ornaments from

rashiTranslations.he: הכלה מחמת האבל כל שלשים יום
rashiTranslations.en: the bride on account of mourning for all thirty days' -

rashiTranslations.he: אלמא כל שלשים בעי' נוי: אם מחמת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: this proves that adornment is required for all thirty days. 'If because of

rashiTranslations.he: סכנת עקרב. מקום שיש לחוש
rashiTranslations.en: danger from a scorpion' - in a place where there is concern

rashiTranslations.he: לעקרבים מותר כל אדם לנעול:
rashiTranslations.en: for scorpions, any person is permitted to wear the sandal.

rashiTranslations.he: בגרעינתה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi begins the next comment ('be-gar'inata' - with its pit), opening the following passage.
